Name: Foundations of Biology I
Description: A biological science course introducing the fundamentals of biology and essential structures, components, and processes of life. Emphasis placed on biochemistry; cell structure, function, organization, and division; sources and uses of biological energy; as well as genetics and inheritance.

Name: Foundations of Biology I Laboratory
Description: To present a study of the general characteristics and basic concepts of living organisms, which includes: the hierarchical organization of life, basic chemistry of life (inorganic and organic), cell types and structures, membrane structure and function, thermodynamics/energy flow, metabolic processes (photosynthesis & cellular respiration), the cell cycle, meiosis, and an introduction to genetics.

Name: Digital Circuit Design
Description: Knowledge and facility in logic design; interfacing digital circuits; Boolean algebra; combinational logic circuits; circuit minimization techniques; flip-flop storage elements; shift registers; counting devices; sequential logic circuits; state machines; computer structure.

Name: Digital Circuit Design Laboratory
Description: Laboratory experiments in the measurement and verification of digital circuits. Discrete and integrated logic circuit design analysis and measurements.

Name: Microprocessor Systems
Description: Microprocessor or microcontroller organizations and programming. Components of a current microprocessor or microcontroller family. Assembly language programming techniques for microprocessors or microcontrollers. General purpose input and output, serial communications, and other peripherals. Hardware and software relationships.

Name: Microprocessor Systems Laboratory
Description: Hands-on experience with a microprocessor is provided through weekly experiments involving hardware and software techniques.

Name: Real-Time Embedded Systems
Description: Overview of real-time embedded systems. Concepts of real-time systems from the user and designer viewpoint. Requirements, design, implementation, and basic properties of real-time embedded application software. Interrupts, concurrent task synchronization, sharing resources, and software reliability.

Name: Computer Architecture
Description: The Von Neuman computer architecture, including processors, memory, input/output, and transfer of information; examples of machine language, assembly language, microprogramming, and operating systems. Advanced topics in computer architecture and computer systems.

Name: English Composition
Description: Adapting communication in response to diverse audiences, purposes, and contexts through rhetorical thinking; addresses composing processes, critical thinking, research, analysis, and documentation. Daytona Beach students must earn a C or better to pass the course.

Name: Speech
Description: A continuation of the study of communication and communication theory with emphasis on overcoming communication apprehension, developing listening skills, mastering oral performance, and writing about communication. Individual sections may focus on public speaking, group discussion, oral interpretation, or interpersonal communication. Section emphasis varies by instructor and is listed in the Schedule of Courses.

Name: Technical Report Writing
Description: This course introduces students to the preparation of formal and informal technical reports, abstracts, proposals, instructions, professional correspondence and other forms of technical communication. Major emphasis is placed on the long technical report and the acquisition of advanced writing skills.

Name: Introduction to Discrete Structures
Description: Fundamental algebraic, logical, and combinatorial concepts of mathematics; foundation for the study of computer science.

Name: Scientific Programming in C
Description: C programming; algorithm development; data types and related operations; input/output operations; control structures; functions; arrays; files; strings.

Name: Computer Science II
Description: Introduction to object-oriented programming, data abstraction, information hiding, algorithm analysis, data structures, exception handling, string processing, recursion, software design and testing. A closed laboratory is included.

Name: Computer Science II Laboratory
Description: Closed laboratory included with CS 225.

Name: Data Structures and Analysis of Algorithms
Description: This course emphasizes the design, implementation, and analysis of algorithms dealing with searching, sorting, graphs, trees, and disk files.

Name: Files and Database Systems
Description: Introduction to file and database systems, theory of database systems, various database models, and the design of a database system. Real-life problems requiring cooperation, problem formulation, and problem-solving skills.

Name: Organization of Programming Languages
Description: Comparative study of programming paradigms. Programming in several languages chosen to illustrate the essential features of the paradigms studied. Formal language concepts introduced.

Name: C Programming and UNIX
Description: Advanced C programming concepts and techniques in the UNIX environment. Operating systems principles, process management, memory management, concurrency, UNIX system programming and tools.

Name: Computer Modeling and Simulation
Description: Modeling and simulation. Statistical models, queuing theory, random variate generation, design and analysis of experiments, and verification and validation of simulation models.

Name: Introduction to Computer Graphics
Description: Computer graphics, algorithms, graphics programming, and graphics design, and graphic packages. Applications to aviation, business, and scientific problems. A term project may be assigned.

Name: Computing Theory
Description: Theoretical foundations of autonomous computation; formal languages; grammars; models of computation; computability and computational complexity; relationships between languages, grammars, and models of computation; properties of languages and classification in the Chomsky Hierarchy.

Name: Operating Systems
Description: Development, structure, and functions of operating systems; demand service models; development of concurrent models.

Name: Information and Computer Security
Description: Overview of information security, including larger context, personnel and operational security, and technical basis and practical difficulties of COMPUSEC itself.

Name: Artificial Intelligence
Description: Introduction to artificial intelligence principles and their applications to real-world problems; agent-based systems; problem-solving algorithms; expert systems; fuzzy logic; feedforward backpropagation artificial neural networks; ethical issues of intelligent systems. Commercial and open-source AI tools and algorithms.

Name: Computer Networks
Description: Modern computer network analysis and design, implementation of the ISO/OSI model, and current standardized protocols. Network architectural layers above the physical layer.

Name: Introduction to Engineering
Description: A project-based, design experience for engineering majors. Introduces the interdisciplinary aspects of engineering through team projects.

Name: Introduction to Computing for Engineers
Description: Introductory course to computer programming. Algorithm design and software development including specification of the problem, design of a solution, implementation of code, and testing. Applies problem-solving approaches to developing algorithms. Data types and related operations; looping; decision; input/output; functions; arrays; and files.

Name: Government of the U.S.
Description: Introduction to basic issues of democracy in the U.S.; constitutional principles; the executive, legislative and judicial branches of government.

Name: Studies in world citizenship
Description: Learning about world citizenship!

Name: Studies in Humanities
Description: Placeholder for actual humanities courses.

Name: 100-200 Lower Level Humanities
Description: Placeholder for 100-200 level humanities courses.

Name: Upper-Level Humanities or Social Sciences
Description: Placeholder for upper-level humanities courses.

Name: Calculus and Analytical Geometry I
Description: Limits and continuity; differentiation and integration of algebraic and elementary transcendental functions; applications of first and second derivatives.

Name: Calculus and Analytical Geometry II
Description: Differentiation and integration of transcendental functions; special integration techniques; applications of the definite integral; numerical methods; infinite series.

Name: Calculus and Analytical Geometry III
Description: Solid analytic geometry; vector functions in three dimensions; partial differentiation; directional derivative and gradient; line integrals; multiple integrals.

Name: Introduction to Scientific Computing
Description: This course is an introduction to the Unix operating system, programming in a high level language (e.g., C or Fortran), and the use of mathematical libraries. Applications may include root-finding algorithms, quadrature, least squares, linear systems, and first order differential equations.

Name: Differential Equations and Matrix Methods
Description: The study of the treatment of ordinary differential equations to include principle types of first and second order equations; methods of substitution on simple higher order equations; linear equations and systems of linear equations with constant coefficients; methods of undetermined coefficients and variation of parameters; Laplace transforms; series solutions; linear algebra and matrix methods of solutions; applications to physics and engineering.

Name: Probability and Statistics
Description: Finite sample spaces; conditional probability and Bayes Theorem, discrete and continuous random variables and their functions; expected value, variance, and standard deviation; systematic study of the major discrete and continuous distributions; moment generating functions; hypothesis testing and estimation.

Name: Software Engineering Practices
Description: Principles and methods of large-scale software engineering; team collaboration on life-cycle projects including planning, requirements, design, implementation, and quality assurance.

Name: Analysis and Design of Software Systems
Description: Methods for analyzing and designing software systems; requirements analysis; modeling system domain, behavior and functionality; software design application; introduction to design patterns.

Name: Software Construction
Description: Advanced programming in an object-oriented language; proficiency in general-purpose software development; software maintenance and refactoring techniques; creating quality code; introduction to tools and processes for significant software environments.

Name: Software Quality Assurance
Description: Key concepts and practices in software testing and quality assurance; software quality through testing, inspection, and walkthrough; testing techniques and methods; management of testing stages; evaluation of different software testing approaches and their advantages and disadvantages.

Name: Software Team Project I
Description: First of two-semester multidisciplinary capstone design course. Requirements gathering, team management, analysis, design, implementation, and testing of software/hardware systems; collaboration using defined processes for product development; project assessment with industrial standards and review techniques.

Name: Software Team Project II
Description: Second of two-semester multidisciplinary capstone design course. Requirements gathering, team management, analysis, design, implementation, and testing of software/hardware systems; collaboration using defined processes for product development; project assessment with industrial standards and review techniques.

Name: Introduction to Space Flight
Description: History of crewed and uncrewed spaceflight; rockets and propulsion theory; orbital mechanics fundamentals; the universe; present and future commercial, industrial, and military applications in space.

Name: Planetary and Space Exploration
Description: This is a survey course of U.S. and international space programs. The student will be introduced to the Earth and its space environment; to methods of scientific exploration; and to spacecraft and payload criteria at the introductory physics level.

Name: Space Transportation System
Description: A survey course of the space transportation system (STS) at the introductory physics level. Included are manned space flight operations, supporting systems, and the space shuttle mission, both present and future. A review of space shuttle flight profiles, guidance and navigation control, proximity operations and rendezvous, and a brief review of hypersonic orbiter aerodynamics are included. Also covered are future STS applications to space station logistical operations, commercial applications, and Department of Defense operations.

Name: Space Station Systems and Operations
Description: This course provides the student with a background in the major aspects of the International Space Station (ISS) and the Russian Mir spacecraft. Specific topics include commercial applications, logistical support, maintenance, servicing, and design concepts.

Name: Life Support Systems
Description: This course is a survey, at the elementary physics level, of the requirements and design considerations for life support systems in space and on other planets. Included are an introduction to basic human physiology, a description of the space environment and a survey of historical life support systems, and a presentation of spacecraft limitations and requirements.

Name: Space Policy and Law - History
Description: Foundational concepts, historical development, and sources of international space policy and law; the U.S. role in developing space policy. Continuing evolution of international space law and policy for space applications; distinguishing public, private and military uses of space; planetary protection; human spaceflight.

Name: Satellite and Spacecraft Systems
Description: Orbital satellites and spacecraft are discussed according to their application, design, and environment. The power system, shielding, and communication systems are reviewed along with their missions, space environment, and limitations.

Name: Spaceflight and Operations Training
Description: Introduction of astronaut and cosmonaut selection and training showing strong parallels with military pilot selection and training, including the rapid evolution of manned space flight training. Current selection techniques, and Spaceflight and Operations Training programs examined, along with the training center facilities and shared training techniques used for International Space Station crews. Examination of commercial training programs already in place and planned.

Name: Russian Space Operations and Technology
Description: Covers Russian space flight efforts including the dramatic race to space. Covers concepts from Tsiolkovsky to today's International Space Station. Highlights Russia's well-known and obscure space programs. Offers insight into the space flight record that is often missing because of the secretive Soviet Union.

Name: Fundamentals of Space Policy and Regulation
Description: Regulatory survey of US law, policy and certification requirements for commercial space programs. Identifying the role of key space authorities and regulators; Licensing elements and approaches for spaceflight operations; distinguishing space regulations for launch/ reentry operations; safety; payloads; and human spaceflight.

Name: Payloads and Integration
Description: Integration and design concepts of spacecraft and payload component subsystems; Space system requirements; Space system performance; Final integrated system validation and testing procedures.

Name: 100-200 Lower Level Social Sciences
Description: Placeholder for actual Social Sciences courses.

Name: College Success
Description: This course is designed to help students assess and develop the personal, interpersonal, intellectual, and social skills necessary to succeed in college. Study skills, Creative/Critical thinking, presentation, goal setting, career concerns and communication skills will be included. This course is available to freshmen only, and is highly recommended for ALL incoming first-year and transfer freshman students.

Name: Survey of Meteorology
Description: This is a survey course in meteorology that includes applications to flight. Included is a systematic development of the following topics: the composition and general structure of the atmosphere, energy and energy transfer, seasonal and daily controls on temperature, pressure, wind, local and regional circulations, atmospheric stability, vertical motion, turbulence, moisture, fog, clouds, precipitation, icing, the general circulation pattern, climate, jet streams, air masses, fronts, mid-latitude cyclones, tropical cyclones, thunderstorms, and weather observations and charts.
